item.title: Electric Texas blues
item.description: Period1942–presentLocationDallas and Houston, later AustinInfluencesAcoustic Texas bluesJazzJump bluesLinksRYM A single-string line that phrases like a horn.

item.title: Smokey Robinson
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, Doo-wop Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1955–present (primary period: 1960–1972) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify When Bob Dylan was asked in a 1965 press conference which poets he admired, he listed Smokey Robinson alongside Rimbaud and Allen Ginsberg — a casual remark that ...

item.title: Gerry Goffin

item.title: Dionne Warwick
item.description: Genres: Pop soul, standards Scene: Brill Building Era: 1962–present (primary period: 1962–1971) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify Listen to the way Dionne Warwick navigates the melody of “Walk on By” — the intervals are wide, and the phrasing syncopates against Burt Bacharach’s rhythm track in ways that m...

item.title: Jeff Barry
item.description: Era: 1960s–1970s (primary period: 1961–1969) Genres: Girl group, Pop soul, Rock & roll, bubblegum Key collaborator: Ellie Greenwich (co-writer and wife, 1962–1965) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify Jeff Barry was the Brill Building’s most instinctive hitmaker — the songwriter who understood, more clea...

item.title: Holland-Dozier-Holland
item.description: Era: 1962–1967 (Motown); 1968–present (independent) Genres: Soul, Pop soul, Girl group Key collaborators: The Supremes, The Four Tops, The Temptations, Martha and the Vandellas RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The most prolific and commercially successful songwriting-production team in the history of Mot...

item.title: Authenticity and its discontents
item.description: Simon Frith’s Performing Rites makes a claim that sounds simple and proves difficult to absorb: when a listener calls a record “authentic,” they are not detecting a quality the music possesses but applying a set of learned conventions about what authentic music should sound like.

item.title: The pop factory
item.description: Theodor Adorno argued in On Popular Music that the culture industry standardizes its products: every hit follows a pre-given pattern, and the variations between individual songs are surface differences designed to mask that uniformity.

item.title: (1953) "Gee"
